metadata.split_role: Expanded porcini, Tylopilus, dark-pored, and brown bolete records.

records.notes: Pale Bitter Bolete: whitish to pale Tylopilus with bitter taste; not a food target.

mushroom_profile.summary: Pale Bitter Bolete: whitish to pale Tylopilus with bitter taste; not a food target.
research_notes: Pale cap, pale pore surface maturing pinkish/brownish, bitter taste, and Tylopilus features are the field clues. Often treated in pine or pine-oak contexts.

records.mushroom_card_note: Pale Bitter Bolete: whitish to pale Tylopilus with bitter taste; not a food target.
records.field_identification: Pale cap, pale pore surface maturing pinkish/brownish, bitter taste, and Tylopilus features are the field clues. Often treated in pine or pine-oak contexts.
records.edibility_detail: Do not put bitter or uncertain Tylopilus/Retiboletus-style boletes in a meal; one bitter specimen can wreck the whole dish.

records.notes: Pallid Bolete: very pale oak-associated bolete; bruising can be inconsistent, so use multiple traits. Image metadata now keeps one verified open-license Mushroom Observer / Commons field image; one older underdocumented supporting image was dropped pending separate rights verification.

mushroom_profile.summary: Pallid Bolete: very pale oak-associated bolete; bruising can be inconsistent, so use multiple traits.
staining: Variable weak blue or pinkish staining
mushroom_profile.season_note: July through September in the Upper Michigan season window; eastern sources describe it as a summer-and-fall oak-associated bolete.
research_notes: Pale cap, smooth white stem, yellowish-to-olive pore surface, oak/hardwood association, and inconsistent bluish/pinkish staining are the checks.

records.mushroom_card_note: Pallid Bolete: very pale oak-associated bolete; bruising can be inconsistent, so use multiple traits.
records.field_identification: Pale cap, smooth white stem, yellowish-to-olive pore surface, oak/hardwood association, and inconsistent bluish/pinkish staining are the checks.
records.edibility_detail: Do not use this record as a food recommendation; treat it as comparison/recognition until reviewed with stronger local evidence.

records.notes: Porphyrellus nebulosus: dark Tylopilus-like bolete with gray-brown tones; included mainly as a cautious comparison species.

mushroom_profile.summary: Porphyrellus nebulosus: dark Tylopilus-like bolete with gray-brown tones; included mainly as a cautious comparison species.
mushroom_profile.season_note: July through September in the Upper Michigan season window; treated conservatively with eastern Tylopilus/Porphyrellus-type summer-and-fall boletes.
research_notes: Still blocked: no species-level non-Wikipedia source and no exact public-usable image verified; keep suppressed from normal cards.
Use the dark cap, darkening pore surface, spore color, host context, and bruising/staining behavior to separate this from other dusky boletes.

records.notes: Porphyrellus sordidus: dark bolete that bruises blue-green to dark; keep out of the “easy edible bolete” bucket.

research_notes: Needs source links and fuller field notes.
Look for the sordid brown cap, dark pore surface/spore-print group, and strong blue-green to dark staining when injured.

records.culinary_uses: Not treated as a food target in this guide; use as an identification/comparison record until the species is better documented locally.

mushroom_profile.summary: Red to purplish Gyroporus-type bolete with brittle flesh and a hollowing stem; included mainly for comparison.
mushroom_profile.season_note: July through September; treated conservatively with other eastern Gyroporus/boletes until species-level Upper Michigan timing is better documented.
research_notes: Still partial: genus-level source only; species-level source and image-rights metadata modernization are still needed for full cleanup.

records.mushroom_card_note: Red to purplish Gyroporus-type bolete with brittle flesh and a hollowing stem; included as an ID/comparison species rather than a prime target.
records.field_identification: Check for Gyroporus traits: brittle/chalky flesh, stem that may become hollow, dry cap, pores rather than gills, and red to purplish tones. Compare against other Gyroporus and small red/brown boletes.
records.edibility_detail: Treat conservatively. Gyroporus species boundaries and local relevance need confirmation before this belongs in any edible target list.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Gyroporus genus overview
use_links.notes: Genus-level support only; species-level source and image-rights modernization remain blockers for full cleanup.

records.culinary_uses: Not recommended because the taste is very bitter.
records.notes: Reddish-brown Bitter Bolete: handsome eastern Tylopilus, but very bitter; useful for ID practice, not the skillet.

mushroom_profile.summary: Reddish-brown Bitter Bolete: handsome eastern Tylopilus, but very bitter; useful for ID practice, not the skillet.
mushroom_profile.season_note: July through September in the Upper Michigan season window; eastern sources describe it as a summer-and-fall hardwood bolete.
research_notes: Purple-brown to reddish-brown cap, pinkish pores, bitter taste, and stem developing olive shades help separate it from mild Tylopilus species.
cap_surface: Purplish brown to reddish brown cap
stem_feature: Brownish stem with possible olive stains

records.notes: Russell's Bolete: yellow-brown to reddish-brown cracked cap and a shaggy/jagged-looking stem; edible reports but not a top-tier target.

mushroom_profile.season_note: July through September in the Upper Michigan season window; treated as a summer-and-fall eastern bolete until local timing is better documented.
research_notes: Still blocked from normal cards: species-level source added, but no exact public-usable image is verified.

records.notes: Spotted/Affine Bolete: hardwood bolete with whitish pores maturing yellow-brown and no dramatic bruising.

mushroom_profile.season_note: July through September in the Upper Michigan season window; eastern sources describe it as a summer-and-fall hardwood bolete.
research_notes: Brown cap sometimes with pale spots, whitish pores becoming yellow-brown, yellow-brown spore print, and no dramatic staining separate it from many olive-spored boletes.

records.culinary_uses: Sometimes considered edible when confidently identified, but this guide treats it as a cautious bolete because Tylopilus look-alikes can be bitter or confusing.

mushroom_profile.summary: Maroon-capped oak-associated Tylopilus with mild taste; useful as a comparison species around bitter boletes.
staining: Pores bruise brown; flesh may slowly stain pinkish
mushroom_profile.season_note: June through September under oaks and other hardwoods.
research_notes: v4.3.178: edible-use source support added from Bolete Filter / Western Pennsylvania Mushroom Club; keep expert-ID and condition cautions.

records.edibility_detail: Do not present as beginner food; confirm maroon cap, pinkish-tan pores, brown-bruising pores, hardwood/oak association, and mild-not-bitter taste.
records.field_identification: Look for a maroon to reddish-brown velvety cap, whitish pores becoming pinkish-tan, brown-bruising pores, and oak/hardwood association. Taste should be mild, not strongly bitter.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Tylopilus badiceps
use_links.notes: Oak association, mild taste, pore color, and brown bruising.

use_links.label: Bolete Filter / Western Pennsylvania Mushroom Club: Tylopilus badiceps
use_links.notes: Supports Michigan presence, mild brown-and-white Tylopilus traits, Tylopilus ferrugineus confusion, oak association, and edible-use as Good.

records.edibility_notes: Mild-tasting Tylopilus; edible reports exist, but confirm it is not a bitter Tylopilus before using.

edible_use.notes: Cook only confirmed mild specimens; skip if bitter, old, buggy, or uncertain.

records.mushroom_card_note: Maroon-capped oak-associated Tylopilus with mild taste; not the bitter-bolete disaster, but still an ID-first bolete.

records.quality_notes: Niche mild Tylopilus with edible-use source support; keep mild-not-bitter and expert-ID warnings. Not a beginner target.
records.eating_use_notes: Cook only confirmed mild specimens; skip if bitter, old, buggy, or uncertain.
records.risk_reason: Risk is confusing mild niche Tylopilus with bitter or poorly understood Tylopilus species.
records.hard_rule: Do not present as beginner food; confirm maroon cap, pinkish-tan pores, brown-bruising pores, hardwood/oak association, and mild-not-bitter taste.

lookalike_risk.label: High — bolete confusion, severe-GI/bitter look-alikes possible
lookalike_risk.short_label: High — bolete confusion, severe-GI/bitter look-alikes possible
lookalike_risk.summary: Edible bolete use needs pore color, staining, host/substrate, taste where appropriate, and separation from false king, bitter Tylopilus, and red-pored/fast-blue-staining caution boletes.
required_checks: Reject bitter taste where safe nibble-and-spit is appropriate
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: Do not eat unidentified boletes; red/orange pores, bitter taste, or violent blue staining mean stop.

dangerous_lookalikes.warning: False king bolete is a northern/Great Lakes porcini-type danger concern.
target_traits: Pale netting near upper stem when expected
lookalike_traits: Hemlock or northern Great Lakes false-king concern
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: If false king traits are possible, do not eat.

target_traits: White/yellow pores without bitter Tylopilus posture
required_checks: Tiny nibble-and-spit only if you already know the mushroom is safe to taste
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Bitter taste means not food; one specimen can ruin the pan.

dangerous_lookalikes.risk: Poisonous / not recommended red-pored blue-staining bolete group.
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Red/orange pores or fast strong blue staining mean stop unless expert-confirmed.
